text: Today I am going to show the robotic assisted pelvic lymphadenectomy. The advantages of robotic technology are there is a stability of the camera and there is an endurance technology it has got. There is a huge magnification with stability. That is a very huge advantage and we have a three dimensional image.

This is a side docking you can see and you can also use a central docking. I am just going to explain you as we go ahead the type of the port placement I am going to do. You can see that the camera you use for robot is very bulky. And in addition to the wide balance and focusing you do, you know because we have a three dimensional image here, it's a binocular vision, we need to use this device to do three dimensional image.
I prefer a V-care instead of the Karl Stoll retrain manipulator because in malignancy I don't want to put a probe into the endometrial canal. So I use a V-care, this is a V-care.

The video shows robotic instruments equipped with endo-wrist technology, allowing 360-degree rotation. Visible instruments include a needle driver and Maryland dissector. The robotic-assisted ports and trocars are 8 mm in size, contrasting with the typical 10 and 12 mm sizes used in other procedures.
The surgeon positions the robotic system close to the patient and marks the uppermost part of the fundus of the uterus as the target organ for camera port placement.

Normally we don't use the umbilicus in case of robot because the robotic instruments are very long. As you can see it must be at least two palm breadth above or eighteen centimeter above the target line. You can either use a varus needle or HACCP technique. I am a great follower of HACCP technique so I do open technique and this is a special trocar which has got a balloon catheter when you use HACCP trocar.
The surgeon inserts a trocar and inflates the bulb to secure it in place, preventing dislodgement. The device is locked to maintain pneumoperitoneum during the procedure.

The surgeon positions the robotic ports, ensuring they are placed at least one palm breadth apart to prevent the robotic arms from collapsing. The ports are positioned approximately fifteen centimeters from the camera port and one centimeter below its level.

You can either use four arms of robot or you can use three arms. So once you put the supraumbilical camera port, unlike the umbilical which is used in laparoscopy, the first port will be fifteen centimeter later. You join all the four lines, this is the quadrangle you get. This is called as no port zone. No assistant ports or any other port should come in the way, in with the quadrangle. This is very important to follow in the principles of robotic surgery.

The surgeon demonstrates the robotic camera's binocular vision capability, using two telescopes for enhanced depth perception. After infiltrating the area with zallocane and bupecane, two robotic ports are inserted alongside the camera port, labeled R-I and R-II, utilizing an ATMM port configuration.

The surgeon positions two robotic ports, R-one on the right side and R-two on the left side, ensuring the small black thick band, which acts as a pivot, is aligned with the abdominal wall. This alignment facilitates the docking of the robotic instruments, allowing them to move effectively around the pivot point.

The surgeon docks the camera port first, securing it with a magnetic strip that holds the trocar snugly, producing a sound upon engagement. The robotic system then docks all instruments, ensuring the trocar remains stable and does not slip during the procedure.

This process is called as the docking of robot. Once the robot is docked, patient cart can never ever be moved. So all the adjustments should be done before. And the position of the patient is extreme tender work. That's why patient needs to be nicely straped so they don't fall.

The robotic surgical setup includes a camera clutch, monopolar cautery, and bipolar cautery, with sensors worn on the surgeon's hand to control the robotic arms. The instruments are positioned together, and the surgeon lifts the uterus, providing a clear view of the surgical field. The video displays a two-dimensional view, while the surgeon experiences a three-dimensional perspective through the robotic system.
